lyricText: There's no such thing as a long wait
note: Annie Duke told Lenny: 'There is no such thing as a long feedback loop.' When VCs claimed their feedback loops were a decade, she challenged them: 'Do you invest and then go to sleep like Rip Van Winkle?' You can always find leading indicators correlated with the outcome you desire.
sourceTitle: This will make you a better decision-maker | Annie Duke

note: Annie Duke's principle for how to approach disagreements and gather honest information in decision-making. Instead of trying to convince people you're right, be genuinely curious about what they see. It's the stance she teaches at First Round Capital for better partner discussions.
sourceTitle: This will make you a better decision-maker | Annie Duke

note: Jeff Bezos's '70% rule': make decisions with 70% of the information you wish you had. Annie Duke discussed this on Lenny's podcast — waiting for more certainty feels like due diligence, but it's often just avoiding the discomfort of being wrong.
sourceTitle: This will make you a better decision-maker | Annie Duke

lyricText: He built the thing for seven years
note: The bridge references Stewart Butterfield and Glitch. Annie told the story on Lenny's podcast: Butterfield built Glitch for years, ran paid marketing for six weeks, did the math, and wrote to investors one Monday morning with 'dead certainty that Glitch was over.' Two days later he pivoted to Slack. As Annie put it: 'As smart as Stewart Butterfield is, he couldn't see Slack until he quit Glitch.'
sourceTitle: This will make you a better decision-maker | Annie Duke

lyricText: A good hand loses, bad hands win
note: A core principle from Annie Duke's poker career and her book Thinking in Bets: outcome quality doesn't equal decision quality. You can play a hand perfectly and still lose. The lesson for product teams is to evaluate decisions by the process, not the result — otherwise you learn the wrong lessons.
sourceTitle: This will make you a better decision-maker | Annie Duke
